140.title: Okinawan Yakuza Splinter Groups
140.summary: This community centers on the Kyokuryū-kai, a yakuza group formed in 1970 from the merger of the Naha Clan and Koza Clan. In 1990, a splinter group, the Okinawa-Kyokuryū-kai, broke away, leading to a prolonged conflict between the two organizations. Both groups are based in Okinawa, Japan, and officially prohibit drug trafficking. The ties primarily reflect organizational splits and mergers, with no cooperation or conflict beyond the internal rivalry.

143.title: Mhallami Mafia Clans in Germany
143.summary: This community consists of four Mhallami crime families based in Germany, primarily the Al-Zein, Miri, and Remmo clans, with ties to the Fakhro clan in Sweden. The Al-Zein and Remmo clans cooperate through a family union, while the Miri clan has a complex relationship with Al-Zein: they are described as allies in some sources, but a murder of an Al-Zein member by the Miri clan indicates underlying conflict. The Al-Zein family also has kinship ties to the Fakhro clan in Malmö. These organizations share a common ethnic origin from the Mhallami people and are involved in drug trafficking, arms trafficking, and other serious crimes across Europe.

144.title: Serbian Mafia and Paramilitary Ties
144.summary: This community centers on the Zemun Clan, a powerful Serbian mafia group, and its connections to paramilitary and other criminal entities. The Zemun Clan cooperated with the Boinas rojas (Red Berets), a paramilitary unit that joined the clan, boosting its strength. It also had a complex relationship with the Surčin clan, initially cooperating in car theft but later escalating to conflict, including an assassination attempt on the Surčin leader. Additionally, the Zemun Clan incorporated members from the Special Operations Unit, a state security force, highlighting the blurring of lines between organized crime and state apparatus in Serbia.

145.title: Brother Speed and Associates
145.summary: This community centers on the Brother Speed Motorcycle Club, an American outlaw motorcycle gang involved in methamphetamine trafficking. Brother Speed cooperates with the Devils Breed and Outsiders motorcycle clubs to acquire precursor chemicals for meth production. In contrast, the club has a violent conflict with the Empties Motorcycle Club, culminating in an attack on Empties members in 2012. The community reflects a network of drug-related alliances and territorial rivalries among smaller outlaw motorcycle clubs.

150.title: Early New York Mafia Rivalries
150.summary: This community consists of early 20th-century New York Mafia factions involved in violent power struggles. The Masseria gang and D'Aquila gang were direct rivals, with Masseria's gunmen assassinating D'Aquila. The Schirò gang also clashed with Masseria, aligning with Maranzano's Castellammarese faction. The Mineo gang was absorbed by D'Aquila, indicating consolidation through coercion. These conflicts reflect the internal warfare that shaped the American Mafia's structure.

153.title: Trinidadian Gang Conflicts
153.summary: This community centers on Rasta City, a Trinidadian gang that split from Muslim City and is locked in violent rivalries with ABG and Sixx. Despite a brief peace deal in 2023, Rasta City remains in conflict with Sixx, which itself splintered from Rasta City. The ties are predominantly conflict-based, reflecting a fragmented landscape of shifting alliances and enmities among Trinidadian gangs.

160.title: UK Drill Gang Rivalries
160.summary: This community centers on the British drill group 67, a Brixton Hill-based collective involved in music and street crime. The network is defined by violent conflicts with rival gangs 150, 37/OJB, and CT, including a fatal shooting of a 150 member by a 67 affiliate and the killing of Cheyon Evans from 37/OJB. These ties highlight the interconnected nature of drill music scenes and gang warfare in London.

164.title: Shan State Army Formation
164.summary: This community consists of four Shan insurgent groups in Myanmar that merged or split to form the Shan State Army (SSA). The Kokang Force and Shan National United Front merged to create the SSA, as did the Shan State Independence Army with the Kokang Force. The Shan State Independence Army also split from Noom Suk Harn, indicating internal fragmentation. These ties reflect a history of unification and division among Shan separatist organizations.

167.title: Myanmar Ethnic Armed Groups
167.summary: This community consists of two ethnic armed organizations and their political wings in Myanmar. The Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA) is the military wing of the Karen National Union (KNU), while the Mon National Liberation Army (MNLA) serves as the armed wing of the New Mon State Party (NMSP). Despite shared goals of ethnic autonomy, the MNLA and KNLA have engaged in minor skirmishes, indicating occasional conflict between these allied groups. The community is bound by organizational ties between political and military branches, but also marked by localized rivalry.

168.title: Philippine IS-affiliated Militant Groups
168.summary: This community consists of four Islamist militant organizations operating in the Philippines, all linked to the Islamic State (IS). The Islamic State – Philippines Province (ISPP) serves as the central node, having emerged from the other three groups: Ansar Khalifa Philippines, the Maute Group, and the Bangsamoro Islamic Freedom Fighters. These 'other' ties indicate that ISPP was formed through the merger or splintering of these pre-existing factions, uniting them under a common IS banner. The community reflects a network of cooperation and organizational lineage, with ISPP acting as the umbrella group for these local insurgent forces.

171.title: Chinese Secret Societies in Malaya
171.summary: This community centers on the Hai San Secret Society, a Hakka-dominated group based in Penang that was deeply involved in the Larut Wars (1862–1873) against the Ghee Hin Kongsi for control of tin mines. The Ghee Hin Kongsi, a rival Cantonese secret society, frequently engaged in violent conflict with Hai San. Hai San also absorbed the Wah Sang Society around 1854 and cooperated with the Tokong Society, which financed tin mining. These ties reflect a network of alliances and rivalries among Chinese secret societies in 19th-century Malaya, driven by economic competition and ethnic divisions.

174.title: North Korean Hacker Network
174.summary: This community centers on the Lazarus Group, a North Korean state-sponsored hacking collective, and its affiliated units AndAriel, Lab 110, and Unit 180. The ties are organizational: Lab 110 is a parent entity, while AndAriel and Unit 180 are subunits or affiliates. Together, they form a hierarchical structure supporting Lazarus Group's global cyber operations for espionage and financial theft.

176.title: Singaporean Gang Rivalries
176.summary: This community centers on Salakau, a prominent Singaporean secret society, which is in conflict with three other groups: 18 Sio Yi Ho, Omega, and Sakongsa. Salakau routinely attacked 18 Sio Yi Ho and Sakongsa as rival gangs, while its conflict with Omega stems from Omega recruiting members from factions of Sio Kun Tong, a group historically opposed to Salakau. Omega, a prison gang turned organized crime group, primarily protects Malay Muslim inmates and engages in drug trafficking. The community is defined by violent turf wars and ethnic tensions, with Salakau as the central aggressor.

177.title: Italian Mafia Clans in Conflict
177.summary: This community consists of four Italian organized crime groups, primarily characterized by ongoing conflicts. The Li Bergolis-Miucci clan is at the center of two rivalries: one with the Romito clan and another with the Lombardi-Ricucci-La Torre clan. Meanwhile, the Granatiero family has cooperative ties with the Romito clan, suggesting an alliance. The network is thus divided into two opposing factions, with the Romito and Granatiero groups aligned against the Li Bergolis-Miucci and Lombardi-Ricucci-La Torre clans.

182.title: Karenni Rebel Factions
182.summary: This community consists of Karenni ethnic armed groups in Myanmar, centered around the Karenni National Progressive Party (KNPP). The KNPP has both cooperative and conflictual ties: its armed wing is the Karenni Army, while the Kayan New Land Party and the Karenni National People's Liberation Front are rival factions. These groups are united by their shared Karenni identity and struggle for autonomy, but divided by political and strategic differences, leading to internal conflicts.

183.title: Russian Cybercrime Syndicate
183.summary: This community centers on Evil Corp, a Russian hacking group known for the Dridex banking trojan, which maintains cooperative ties with other cybercriminal entities. Evil Corp has a personal friendship with a core member of Jabber Zeus, indicating direct collaboration. It also potentially uses Raspberry Robin's infrastructure for attacks, suggesting a symbiotic relationship. Additionally, Necurs is listed as an author of Dridex, linking it to Evil Corp through development ties. These connections form a network of Russian cybercriminals specializing in financial theft and malware distribution.

187.title: Boston Street Gang Network
187.summary: This community centers on the Lucerne Street Doggz, a violent African American street gang in Boston's Mattapan neighborhood, involved in drug trafficking and shootings. The Doggz have one ally, the Mascot Street Killas, and two rivals, the Morse Street County and Norfolk Street Bulls. The ties reflect a local gang rivalry and alliance structure typical of Boston's street gang landscape.

190.title: Bihar Caste-Based Dacoit Gangs
190.summary: This community consists of four caste-based dacoit gangs active in Bihar, India, during the 1980s, primarily in the Kaimur plateau region. The central organization, the Ramashish Koeri gang, cooperated with the Ghamari Karwar, Rambachan Yadav, and Triveni Kahar gangs in committing murders. These gangs emerged from a context of extreme poverty and feudal repression, with members from Scheduled Castes and backward castes. Their cooperation suggests a network of allied criminal groups operating under similar socio-economic conditions.

193.title: Slovak Organized Crime Rivalries
193.summary: This community consists of four Slovak criminal groups: Pápayovci, Sátorovci, Sýkorovci, and Černákovci. The central group, Pápayovci, had cooperative ties with both Sýkorovci and Černákovci, indicating alliances or partnerships. However, they were in conflict with Sátorovci, who ultimately eliminated them in 1999. The community is defined by a mix of cooperation among some groups and violent rivalry with others, reflecting the competitive nature of organized crime in Slovakia.

194.title: Hells Angels Chapter Network
194.summary: This community consists of four Hells Angels chapters from the US and UK, linked by sponsorship and merger ties. The Oakland chapter sponsored the South London chapter, while the San Francisco chapter sanctioned the East London chapter. Subsequently, the East London and South London chapters merged into a single entity. These relationships reflect the organizational structure and expansion of the Hells Angels motorcycle club across continents.

195.title: San Francisco Street Gangs
195.summary: This community consists of African-American street gangs based in San Francisco, primarily centered around Westmob, which has ties to Oakdale Mob and Sunnydale Gangsters through cooperation. Westmob is engaged in a long-running conflict with Big Block, a rivalry documented since 1999. The gangs are involved in drug trafficking and have connections to rap music, with their activities concentrated in neighborhoods like Hunters Point.

199.title: Bakassi Separatist Network
199.summary: This community centers on the Bakassi Movement for Self-Determination (BAMOSD), a separatist group seeking independence for the Bakassi peninsula. BAMOSD receives support from two organizations, NDDSC and SCAPO, indicating a cooperative network of backing. The Bakassi Freedom Fighters are the radical armed faction of BAMOSD, representing an internal organizational split. The community is united by the common goal of Bakassi self-determination, with cooperation among supporters and a constituent relationship with the militant wing.

136.title: 1947 Hollister Riot Motorcycle Clubs
136.summary: This community consists of three outlaw motorcycle clubs—Boozefighters, Market Street Commandos, and Pissed Off Bastards of Bloomington—that are historically linked by their joint participation in the 1947 Hollister riot. The incident, which inspired the film The Wild One, involved all three clubs cooperating in the event. The Market Street Commandos later merged with the Hells Angels in 1954, while the Pissed Off Bastards of Bloomington is considered a precursor to the Hells Angels. Their ties are exclusively cooperative, centered on this shared historical event.

148.title: Galician Drug Trafficking Rivalries
148.summary: This community centers on the Galician drug trafficking clan Los Charlines, which is locked in a violent feud with the rival Os Caneos group. Despite this local conflict, Los Charlines maintains a cooperative alliance with the Sicilian Corleonesi clan, likely for drug trafficking operations. The community highlights the intersection of local Galician criminal networks with international organized crime, particularly the Sicilian Mafia.

154.title: Guatemala-Mexico Border Drug Rivalry
154.summary: This community revolves around a violent power struggle for control of drug trafficking routes along the Guatemala-Mexico border. Los Huistas, a powerful Guatemalan cartel based in Huehuetenango, have long dominated the region, but their imposition of taxes on Tío Balde sparked tensions. In response, Tío Balde allied with the CJNG to form the Cartel Chiapas-Guatemala, a new organization explicitly created to challenge Los Huistas' dominance. The community is defined by this central conflict, with cooperation between Tío Balde and CJNG fueling a direct rivalry against Los Huistas.

159.title: Sydney Assyrian Gang Conflicts
159.summary: This community centers on DLASTHR, an Assyrian criminal organization in southwestern Sydney, Australia, involved in drug trafficking and violence. DLASTHR is in conflict with the True Kings over drug supply, and also has a rivalry with the Bronx Boys. The ties are exclusively conflict-based, highlighting turf wars and competition for drug markets.

165.title: Filipino Gang Rivalries
165.summary: This community centers on the Sigue Sigue Sputnik, a major Filipino street gang, and its conflicts with two other groups: the Sigue Sigue Commandos and the Oxo group. The Sputniks and Commandos are described as ardent rivals, with documented clashes including the 2020 New Bilibid Prison riots where Sputniks fought an alliance of Commandos and Abu Sayyaf. Additionally, the Sputniks view the Oxo group as invaders and enemies, as reflected in their motto. These ties are exclusively conflictual, highlighting a network of gang warfare in the Philippines.

172.title: Buffalo Street Gang Rivalries
172.summary: This community consists of three street gangs based in Buffalo, New York: the Bailey Boys, LRGP, and Milla Gangsta Bloods. The Bailey Boys are central, with conflicts against both LRGP and Milla Gangsta Bloods, indicating a network of rivalries. LRGP and the Bailey Boys are direct opponents, engaged in violent disputes over drug territory. The Milla Gangsta Bloods also oppose the Bailey Boys, suggesting a broader pattern of gang warfare in Buffalo. These organizations are tied primarily through conflict, with no cooperation or kinship ties present.

178.title: Verde Clan Conflicts
178.summary: This community consists of three criminal clans—Verde, Puca, and Ranucci—linked exclusively by conflict. The Verde clan is central, having fought with both the Ranucci and Puca clans. A notable incident involved a shootout between the Verde and Puca clans that resulted in a Puca member's death. These violent rivalries define the community's structure, with no cooperative or organizational ties present.

179.title: Nuova Camorra Speciale and Allies
179.summary: This community centers on the Nuova Camorra Speciale (NCS), a powerful Neapolitan crime organization, which has both cooperative and conflictual ties with other local clans. The NCS cooperated with the Clan Belforte, as the Belforte clan joined the NCS, indicating a strategic alliance or merger. In contrast, the NCS was in conflict with the Orefice clan, threatening them as part of a broader rivalry with the Vesuvian Camorra. The community thus reflects the NCS's efforts to expand its influence through both alliances and intimidation.

180.title: White Supremacist Network
180.summary: This community consists of three white supremacist organizations based in the United States: White Aryan Resistance (WAR), American Front, and East Side White Pride. WAR and American Front had a cooperative relationship starting in 1988, though tensions arose after Aryan Woodstock. East Side White Pride allegedly had ties to WAR, indicating a broader network of white supremacist groups. The community is characterized by cooperation and shared ideology, despite internal disagreements.

181.title: Edmonton Vietnamese Gang Succession
181.summary: This community centers on the Crazy Dragons, a street gang in the Canadian Prairies that emerged from the earlier Trang gang. The Crazy Dragons have a cooperative tie with Fresh Off the Boat, supplying them with firearms. The Trang gang is linked as a predecessor, having folded and been succeeded by the Crazy Dragons. These ties reflect a lineage and ongoing criminal cooperation among Vietnamese-influenced gangs in the region.

184.title: LASD Deputy Gangs Network
184.summary: This community consists of three deputy gangs within the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department: the Compton Executioners, the Lynwood Vikings, and the 2000 Boys. The Compton Executioners and Lynwood Vikings are explicitly allied, as indicated by their infoboxes listing each other as allies. The Compton Executioners were founded by a former member of the 2000 Boys, establishing a historical organizational link. These groups are connected through cooperation and shared origins, all involved in police corruption and misconduct. The Lynwood Vikings are noted for neo-Nazi, white supremacist ideology, while the Compton Executioners have been linked to violence and congressional scrutiny.

186.title: Vietnamese Gang Mentorship and Conflict
186.summary: This community centers on Đại Cathay's gang, which has both cooperative and conflictual ties with two other Vietnamese gangs. Năm Cam's organization learned casino business methods from Đại Cathay, indicating a mentorship or influence relationship. In contrast, Tín Mã Nàm's gang fought a gang war with Đại Cathay's gang in 1964, but later negotiated and ceded territory, suggesting a resolution of conflict. The community is thus defined by a mix of cooperation through knowledge transfer and conflict followed by territorial settlement.

188.title: Early 20th Century NYC Gang Rivalries
188.summary: This community consists of three early 20th-century New York City gangs—Kaplan, Orgen, and Spanish—that were deeply entangled in violent conflicts over labor slugging operations and shifting alliances. The Kaplan and Spanish gangs initially cooperated but later became bitter rivals, engaging in a violent war. Meanwhile, the Kaplan and Orgen gangs fought over control of labor slugging, a form of union-related violence. These ties highlight a volatile network of temporary alliances and deadly competition for criminal dominance in the city's underworld.

189.title: Amsterdam Gang Alliances and Rivalries
189.summary: This community centers on the Hopi Boys, an Afro-Curaçaoan gang in Amsterdam, who are allied with the Chinese Green Gang, a historical triad from Shanghai. The Hopi Boys are in active conflict with the Kloekhorststraat Gang over turf and rivalry. The Green Gang's involvement suggests a cross-continental cooperation, likely in drug trafficking or other illicit enterprises. The Kloekhorststraat Gang's lack of description indicates it may be a local Amsterdam group, making this a mix of local and international organized crime ties.

191.title: Liverpool Gang Rivalries
191.summary: This community consists of three Liverpool-based gangs—Crocky Crew, Norris Green Strand Crew, and Strand Gang—locked in violent conflicts. The Crocky Crew killed a member of the Norris Green Strand Crew, sparking a deadly feud. Additionally, the Crocky Crew and Strand Gang were engaged in a violent war involving multiple shootings and murders, which tragically resulted in the death of an innocent child. These ties are exclusively conflictual, highlighting a pattern of territorial and retaliatory violence among these groups.

196.title: Chicago Kidnappers and Mob Ties
196.summary: This community centers on the College Kidnappers, a group of University of Illinois alumni who kidnapped wealthy mobsters for ransom in early 1930s Chicago. They specifically targeted the Al Capone mob, which attempted to negotiate payoffs to stop the kidnappings, indicating a cooperative yet adversarial relationship. Additionally, the group's leader, Theodore 'Handsome Jack' Klutas, had connections with John Dillinger's gang, linking these criminal enterprises through cooperation. The community thus reflects a network of organized crime figures in Chicago, bound by kidnapping and ransom activities.

197.title: Calabrian 'Ndrangheta Allied Families
197.summary: This community consists of the 'Ndrina Rodà, a 'ndrina of the 'Ndrangheta from Condofuri, Calabria, and its allied families, Nucera and Casile. The Rodà operate along the Ionian coast and have expanded into Liguria, specifically in Lavagna, where they cooperate with the Nucera and Casile families. Their activities include public works corruption, such as influencing contracts for the Strada statale 106 Jonica. Recent law enforcement operations have targeted their operations in Liguria, highlighting the group's ongoing criminal influence.

198.title: Baltimore Crew Leadership Families
198.summary: This community centers on the Baltimore Crew, an Italian-American organized crime group active in Baltimore from the 1920s to the 1990s. The crew was originally led by the D'Urso family before the Corbi family took over functional leadership after 1920. Both families are tied to the crew through organizational relationships, reflecting a succession of control within the same criminal enterprise.

206.title: Sinopoli Feud Allies
206.summary: This community consists of three organizations—Filleti, De Angelis, and Orfeo—united by their alliance against the Alvaro-Violi-Macrì group during the Sinopoli feud. The ties between Filleti and De Angelis, and Filleti and Orfeo, are cooperative, indicating a shared opposition to a common enemy. The community is defined by this strategic partnership, with Filleti serving as a central node connecting the other two. No conflict or organizational relationships exist among them, highlighting their cohesive front in the feud.

207.title: Aryan Empire Formation
207.summary: This community consists of three white supremacist organizations: the 211 Crew, the American Nazi Party, and the Aryan Syndicate. The 211 Crew, a violent prison gang based in the United States, merged with both the Aryan Syndicate and the American Nazi Party to form a unified entity known as the Aryan Empire. These mergers indicate a consolidation of far-right extremist groups, combining their resources and membership for greater influence. The community is characterized by cooperation through organizational mergers rather than conflict.

209.title: Bristol Drug Turf War
209.summary: This community centers on the Aggi Crew, a Bristol-based drug gang that dominated the local market in the 1990s until key members were jailed. Upon release, they attempted to reclaim territory from Yardie gangs, including the Hype Crew and Mountain View Posse, by demanding tax payments. This sparked a violent turf war involving shootings, ultimately leading to the Aggi Crew's re-arrest. The ties are exclusively conflict-driven, reflecting a struggle for control over Bristol's drug trade.

210.title: Ciminà Feud Allies
210.summary: This community consists of three organizations—Barillaro, Romano, and Zucco—united by their alliance against the Varacalli-Franco-Polifroni-Spagnolo group during the Ciminà feud. The ties between Romano and Barillaro, and Barillaro and Zucco, are cooperative, indicating a shared opposition to a common rival. Despite the lack of direct ties between Romano and Zucco, their mutual connection through Barillaro suggests a cohesive coalition. The community is defined by this conflict-driven cooperation, with all members likely operating in the same region.

211.title: Santa Ana Gang Rivalries
211.summary: This community centers on Lopers 13, a Mexican American street gang in Santa Ana, California, which is in conflict with two other local gangs, Logan Street and Walnut Street. The ties are exclusively rivalries, indicating a network of territorial or criminal competition. Lopers 13 is the most central organization, with ties to both rivals, while Logan Street and Walnut Street have no direct connection to each other. The community reflects the fragmented and adversarial nature of street gang dynamics in Santa Ana.

212.title: Polish Mafia Rivalries and Splits
212.summary: This community centers on the Pruszków mafia, a dominant Polish criminal organization from the 1990s, and its relationships with two other groups. The Mokotów group splintered from the Pruszków mafia in 2000, indicating an internal split. The Wołomin mafia is listed as a rival, suggesting conflict over territory or operations. These ties highlight fragmentation and competition within Polish organized crime.

214.title: Motorcycle Club Puppet Network
214.summary: This community centers on the Patriots Motorcycle Club, which was created as a puppet club by the Hells Angels MC Kent chapter. The Patriots originated from the Soldier Blue Motorcycle Club, which was renamed to form the Patriots. These ties indicate a hierarchical relationship where the Hells Angels exert control through the Patriots, while the Soldier Blue club was absorbed into this structure. The community reflects a pattern of organized crime groups using front or support clubs to expand influence.

215.title: Lyari Gang Rivalries and Succession
215.summary: This community centers on the People's Aman Committee (PAC), a Pakistani militant group and organized crime gang based in Lyari, Karachi, involved in drug trafficking and extortion. The PAC is in conflict with the Arshad Pappu gang, reflecting the violent gang wars in Lyari. Additionally, the PAC was renamed to the Revenge Youth Organisation (RYO), indicating an organizational succession. These ties highlight a mix of rivalry and rebranding within Karachi's criminal underworld.

216.title: Mumbai Dons Cooperation Network
216.summary: This community consists of three historical Mumbai-based organized crime figures: Haji Mastan, Karim Lala, and Varadarajan Mudaliar. The ties are exclusively cooperative, with Haji Mastan acting as a central connector. He worked in association with Karim Lala and collaborated with Varadarajan Mudaliar to steal dock cargo. The network reflects a period of cooperation among these prominent gang leaders in Mumbai's underworld.

217.title: Fullerton Korean-American Gang Origins
217.summary: This community centers on the Fullerton Boys, a Korean-American street gang in Fullerton, California, founded in 1996. The gang's membership originated from two other gangs, LVM and SZA, which are also part of this community. The ties between Fullerton Boys and LVM and SZA are constituent relationships, indicating that Fullerton Boys formed from members of these predecessor gangs. This community reflects a lineage of gang evolution in Fullerton, where disbanded groups coalesced into a new collective identity.

218.title: Toronto Gang Conflicts and Subsets
218.summary: This community centers on the Five Point Generalz, a prominent Canadian street gang in Toronto, and its ties to two other groups. G2M is a younger subset of the Five Point Generalz, indicating an organizational relationship. In contrast, the Five Point Generalz are in conflict with the Baghdad Crew, as evidenced by a shootout that killed a bystander. The community thus reflects both internal factional ties and external rivalries.

220.title: Rival Flag Armies in Vietnam
220.summary: This community consists of three Chinese-led mercenary armies active in late 19th-century Vietnam: the Black Flag Army, White Flag Army, and Yellow Flag Army. The Black Flag Army, founded by Liu Yongfu, fought against both the White Flag Army and the Yellow Flag Army, the latter being used by the Vietnamese court to counterbalance the Black Flags. These conflicts reflect the fragmented and competitive nature of these bandit groups, which operated in northern Vietnam and were involved in local power struggles and resistance against French colonialism.

221.title: Ciarelli Clan Rivalry Cluster
221.summary: This community centers on the Clan dei Ciarelli, which is in conflict with both the Famiglia Di Rocco and the Famiglia Spinelli. The ties are exclusively conflictual, stemming from a 1972 ambush by the Ciarelli clan against the other two families. The Di Rocco and Spinelli families are allied against the Ciarelli clan, but no direct ties exist between them in this network. The community represents a localized feud within organized crime.

222.title: Russian Mafia and Drug Cartel
222.summary: This community centers on the Izmaylovskaya gang, a prominent Russian organized crime group, which maintains cooperative ties with both the Podolskaya gang and the Cali KGB Cartel. The Izmaylovskaya gang has good relations with the Podolskaya gang, another Russian criminal group, suggesting a local alliance. Additionally, the Cali KGB Cartel supplied cocaine to the Izmaylovskaya gang, indicating a transnational drug trafficking partnership. These connections highlight a network of cooperation between Russian mafia groups and a Colombian cartel.

224.title: Congolese Rebel Cooperation Network
224.summary: This community consists of three Congolese rebel groups that cooperated during the Second Congo War. The Congolese Rally for Democracy–Goma (RCD-Goma) served as a central node, cooperating with both Mudundu 40 / Front de Résistance et de Défense du Kivu and the Mouvement de Lutte contre l'Agression au Zaïre / Forces Unies de Résistance Nationale contre l'Agression de la République Démocratique du Congo. These alliances were likely formed to oppose the Congolese government and its foreign backers, reflecting the fragmented and shifting loyalties characteristic of the conflict.

225.title: Italian Mafia Marriage Alliances
225.summary: This community consists of three Italian organized crime families—Mercuri, Corica, and Guerrisi—connected through marriage and association. The Mercuri and Guerrisi families united via a marriage in the 1920s, while Mercuri also maintained ties with the Corica family. These cooperative bonds suggest a network of kinship and alliance typical of traditional mafia structures, likely facilitating mutual support and shared criminal enterprises.

226.title: Young Hacker Collectives Collaboration
226.summary: This community consists of three hacking groups—Scattered Spider, ALPHV, and Lapsus$—united by cooperative ties. Scattered Spider collaborated with ALPHV on ransomware operations, and later formed a collective with Lapsus$ and ShinyHunters in 2025. These groups are primarily composed of young, English-speaking hackers known for social engineering and extortion attacks. Their cooperation suggests a merging of tactics and resources within the cybercriminal underground.

227.title: Racial Gang Conflicts in LA
227.summary: This community centers on the Spook Hunters, a white racist youth gang in 1940s-1950s South Gate, California, whose attacks on black youths prompted the formation of two protective gangs: The Slausons and The Gladiators. The ties are exclusively conflict-driven, with the Spook Hunters as aggressors and the other two gangs forming in self-defense. The community reflects racial tensions and retaliatory gang formation in mid-20th century Los Angeles.

228.title: Detroit Italian Mob Feud
228.summary: This community consists of three Italian-American gangs in Detroit: the Gianola brothers, the Adamo gang, and the Vitale gang. The Gianola brothers were involved in violent conflicts with both the Adamo gang over racket control and the Vitale gang in a war for dominance of Detroit's Italian underworld. These rivalries define the community, highlighting a fragmented and competitive criminal landscape.

163.title: Castellammarese War Rivals
163.summary: This community consists of two rival Mafia factions, the Castellammarese clan and the Masseria crime family, which were locked in a violent conflict known as the Castellammarese War. The war was a power struggle for control of the Italian-American Mafia in New York City during the late 1920s and early 1930s. All three ties between them are conflict edges, reflecting the intense hostility and competition that defined their relationship. This community represents a classic example of organized crime rivalry, where territorial and leadership disputes led to open warfare.

173.title: Milanese Gang Rivalry and Alliance
173.summary: This community consists of two Italian criminal groups active in Milan during the 1970s and 1980s: the Banda della Comasina and the Turatello Crew. Their relationship evolved from a violent gang war into a strategic alliance, reflecting the shifting dynamics of organized crime in the region. The Banda della Comasina, led by Renato Vallanzasca, was involved in robberies, kidnappings, and weapons trafficking, while the Turatello Crew was a rival faction. Despite initial conflict, they later cooperated, likely to consolidate power or share resources.

185.title: Niger Delta Militant Rivalry
185.summary: This community consists of two rival militant groups from the Niger Delta region: the Niger Delta People's Volunteer Force (NDPVF) and the Niger Delta Vigilante (NDV). Their relationship is defined by intense conflict over control of fuel supply routes, with the NDV receiving government protection and being used as a paramilitary force to eliminate the NDPVF. The three ties all indicate a deep-seated rivalry and violent confrontation between the two organizations.

192.title: Italian Mafia Clan Succession
192.summary: This community consists of two Italian organized crime groups, Clan Cappellari-Caputo and Clan Piarulli-Ferraro, linked by a direct succession relationship. Clan Piarulli-Ferraro is described as the direct continuation of Clan Cappellari-Caputo, indicating a transformation or rebranding rather than a separate entity. The ties are classified as 'other,' reflecting an organizational or kinship-based connection rather than cooperation or conflict. This suggests a single criminal lineage that has evolved over time.

200.title: Buffalo Gang Rivalry
200.summary: This community consists of two rival street gangs from Buffalo, New York: the Central Park Gang and the Fruit Belt Gang. The Fruit Belt Gang, founded in the 1960s-1970s, is primarily African American and involved in drug trafficking, weapons smuggling, and murder. The two gangs are in direct opposition, with ongoing violent conflicts including shootings and retaliatory murders. Their relationship is defined solely by conflict, with no cooperation or organizational ties.

201.title: Jewish Sex Trafficking Syndicates
201.summary: This community consists of two closely related Jewish criminal organizations, Zwi Migdal and its splinter group Ashkenazum, both active in early 20th-century Argentina. They were primarily involved in sex trafficking of Eastern European Jewish women and forced prostitution, with Zwi Migdal being the larger and more established syndicate. Ashkenazum broke away from Zwi Migdal, yet the two groups are listed as allies, indicating cooperation despite the split. Their shared criminal focus and historical ties define this community as a network of organized crime centered on human trafficking.

202.title: Philadelphia Mafia Warring Factions
202.summary: This community consists of two rival factions from the Philadelphia crime family: the Merlino faction and the Stanfa faction. They were locked in a violent conflict known as the Third Philadelphia Mafia War, which involved a series of murders and power struggles. The Merlino faction, led by Joseph 'Skinny Joey' Merlino, challenged the leadership of John Stanfa, leading to a bloody feud in the 1990s. The conflict ultimately resulted in the imprisonment of key figures and the reorganization of the Philadelphia underworld.

203.title: Mahdi Army Successor Network
203.summary: This community consists of the Mahdi Army and its successor, the Peace Companies. The Mahdi Army, an Iraqi Shia militia, was reorganized into the Peace Companies, which operate as a more politically integrated force. The ties between them are organizational, reflecting a direct lineage and transformation rather than cooperation or conflict.

204.title: Buffalo Street Gang Rivalry
204.summary: This community consists of two rival Hispanic street gangs from Buffalo, New York: the 10th Street Gang and the 7th Street Gang. Both gangs are involved in drug trafficking and violent crime, with a particularly intense conflict between them that resulted in 38 homicides between 2009 and 2012. The rivalry is the defining feature of this community, as there are no cooperative or organizational ties. The 10th Street Gang, formed in the 1980s, is multicultural with a Hispanic majority, while the 7th Street Gang was founded in 2000 by Efrain Hidalgo. Both gangs faced significant law enforcement action, with mass arrests on RICO charges in 2011 and 2017, respectively.

205.title: Greco Clan Rivalry
205.summary: This community consists of two Sicilian Mafia factions, the Ciaculli Greco clan and the Croceverde Giardini Greco clan, both part of the larger Greco family. They are connected solely through conflict, having fought each other in the 1940s. Their rivalry is historically noted, indicating a long-standing feud within the same criminal lineage.

208.title: Russian Cocaine and Counterfeit Network
208.summary: This community consists of two Russian criminal groups, the Komarovskaya organized criminal group and the Usvyatsov-Putyrsky gang, bound by strong cooperative ties. The Usvyatsov-Putyrsky gang provides critical support to the Komarovskaya OPG in cocaine supply and counterfeit dollar trade. Their close working relationship suggests a symbiotic partnership focused on drug trafficking and financial crimes.

213.title: Hells Angels Cocaine Network
213.summary: This community consists of two chapters of the Hells Angels Motorcycle Club: the London chapter and the Montreal chapter. The tie between them is cooperative, involving the shipment of cocaine from Montreal to London. This indicates an internal supply chain within the organization, highlighting the club's involvement in drug trafficking across international borders.

219.title: VVT vs AK Aannan Rivalry
219.summary: This community consists of two rival Canadian Tamil street gangs, VVT and AK Aannan, both originating from Sri Lankan Tamil immigrant communities in Toronto. Their relationship is defined by a long-running violent conflict that peaked from the mid-1990s to early 2000s, punctuated by a brief truce in 1998 that collapsed after a murder in 1999. VVT, founded in the early 1990s and named after a town in Sri Lanka, was involved in drug trafficking, fraud, weapons trafficking, counterfeiting, and extortion, but is now largely inactive. The rivalry between these two gangs shaped much of the Tamil gang violence in Toronto during that period.

223.title: Brazilian Skinhead Factions
223.summary: This community consists of two Brazilian skinhead groups, Carecas do ABC and Carecas do Subúrbio, linked by a split where Carecas do ABC emerged as a dissident faction from Carecas do Subúrbio. Carecas do ABC, founded in 1987, is a nationalist and anti-communist group that accepts Afro-Brazilian members but uses Nazi symbolism, and has been involved in violent crimes. The relationship is defined by organizational schism rather than ongoing cooperation or conflict.

229.title: Sicilian Mafia Feud
229.summary: This community consists of two Sicilian Mafia families, the Matranga crime family and the Provenzano family, locked in a violent conflict. The Matranga brothers engaged in extortion and intimidation against the Provenzano family, escalating into a full-scale gang war. Despite their shared criminal background, their relationship is defined by rivalry rather than cooperation. This feud reflects the internal power struggles typical of organized crime networks.

230.title: Ndrangheta Locali in Calabria
230.summary: This community consists of two 'Ndrangheta organizations from Calabria, Italy: the Mandamento tirrenico and the Società di Polistena. The Società di Polistena is a historical 'ndrina based in Polistena, involved in drug and arms trafficking, while the Mandamento tirrenico is a higher-level district that coordinates local clans. Their sole tie is a dependency relationship, with the Società di Polistena being subordinate to the Mandamento tirrenico, reflecting the hierarchical structure of the 'Ndrangheta.

231.title: Ndrangheta High-Rank Structure
231.summary: This community consists of two closely related organizations within the 'Ndrangheta, the Calabrian mafia: Santa and Vangelo. Vangelo is a higher rank within the Società maggiore, positioned above Santa. Both are part of the same hierarchical structure, with Santa being a high-level criminal organization founded in the mid-1970s. The tie between them is an organizational relationship, indicating a clear chain of command within the mafia's upper echelons.
